title: How the striatum encodes avoidance behaviors after repeated trauma

htmlContent:
The body's shortcut to survival

I want to tell you about a small structure deep in your brain. It's called the striatum. And honestly, it might be running more of your life than you realize.

Here's the thing. When you've been through repeated trauma, your brain doesn't just learn new things the way other brains do. It learns shortcuts. Survival shortcuts. And the striatum is ground zero for this kind of learning.

Think about what happens when you touch a hot stove. You pull your hand back before you even feel the pain. That's your striatum at work, encoding a simple avoidance behavior. One event, one lesson, one quick response. But what happens when the hot stove follows you around? When it shows up at the dinner table, in your bedroom, in the way someone looks at you?

That's where things get complicated.

Repeated trauma doesn't teach your brain one simple lesson. It teaches your brain that danger is everywhere. And the striatum, being the diligent little structure it is, starts encoding avoidance behaviors for everything. For sounds. For smells. For the way someone clears their throat. For the feeling of being seen.

I've been there. I know what it's like to have your body make decisions before your conscious mind even registers a threat. It's exhausting. And it's not your fault.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals in the wild shake off trauma. They don't develop chronic avoidance patterns. They don't get stuck. But humans do. And a big part of that stuckness lives in the striatum.

Here's how it works. The striatum is part of the basal ganglia, a set of structures involved in habit formation, movement, and reward. When you repeat a behavior, especially one that reduces fear or pain, the striatum strengthens the neural pathway for that behavior. It's like a path in the woods. The more you walk it, the more worn it becomes. Eventually, it becomes the only path you can see.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

For someone with complex trauma, that path is avoidance. Avoiding eye contact. Avoiding conflict. Avoiding intimacy. Avoiding your own body. Each time you avoid, the striatum says, "Good job. We survived. Let's do that again."

Stay with me here, because this is where it gets personal. Your striatum doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a remembered one. It doesn't know that the abuse ended years ago. It just knows that certain cues were followed by danger, and avoidance worked. So it keeps encoding. Keeps reinforcing. Keeps pulling you away from life.

And the worst part? The avoidance itself becomes a source of suffering. You start avoiding the feeling of wanting to avoid. You feel shame about your own survival strategies. You call yourself broken. But you're not broken. You're a person whose striatum learned a very specific, very thorough lesson about how to stay alive.

What the research actually shows

I'm not making this up. There's real neuroscience behind this.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown that trauma alters the brain's ability to distinguish between past and present danger. The striatum, along with the amygdala and prefrontal cortex, gets locked into a pattern of hypervigilance and avoidance. It's not a choice. It's a neurological adaptation.

Studies using fMRI scans have found that people with PTSD show increased activity in the striatum when exposed to trauma-related cues. Their brains are actively working to avoid something that isn't there anymore. And this isn't just a psychological issue. It's a biological one.

Wild, right? Your brain is literally wired to avoid things that aren't happening.

But here's what I want you to hear. That wiring can change. Neuroplasticity is real. The striatum can learn new patterns. It just takes time, patience, and the right kind of input.

One of the most effective ways to start rewiring the striatum is through somatic approaches. Body-based therapies that help you gently approach what you've been avoiding, rather than running from it. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). It gives you a map for understanding how your nervous system works and how to work with it instead of against it.

Here's what I want you to notice right now. As you're reading this, your nervous system is doing something. Maybe your shoulders crept up toward your ears. Maybe your breath got shallow. That's not a problem - that's information. Your body is telling you something about how safe it feels to take this in.

Stephen Porges talks about this in his polyvagal work. The nervous system doesn't wait for your conscious mind to decide whether something is threatening. It's already made that call before you finish reading a sentence. Your neuroception - that below-conscious threat detection system - is running 24/7. It doesn't take breaks. It doesn't sleep. And it was shaped by every experience you've ever had, especially the ones you can't remember.

I think about this a lot. How much of what we call "personality" is actually just a nervous system doing its best with the wiring it got. How much of what we call "overreacting" is actually a perfectly calibrated response to a world that once was genuinely dangerous. Your wiring isn't broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Peter Levine's work on how animals complete the stress response cycle is foundational for understanding why we get stuck and how to move through it.

The trap of toxic positivity

I need to be honest with you about something. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage here.

You've probably heard phrases like "just think positive" or "you create your own reality" or "what you resist persists." These are not helpful. In fact, they can be deeply harmful for someone whose striatum has encoded avoidance as a survival strategy.

Here's why. When someone tells you to "just stop avoiding," they're asking you to override a biological system that has kept you alive. That's not empowering. That's dangerous. Your striatum isn't going to listen to a platitude. It's going to do what it knows works.

I've had clients tell me they felt like failures because they couldn't "just let go" of their trauma. They'd read books about ation and thought their avoidance was a character flaw. It's not. It's a brain structure doing its job.

Gabor Mate, who writes about trauma and addiction, says it plainly: "Don't ask why the addiction, ask why the pain." The same applies to avoidance. Don't ask why you can't stop avoiding. Ask why avoidance made sense in the first place. Because it did. At some point, it kept you safe.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). It's about how the body holds trauma and how we can gently, safely release it. No toxic positivity. Just honest, grounded work.

Rewiring without force

So how do you actually change the striatum's patterns without forcing yourself into more trauma?

It starts with safety. Real safety. Not the kind of safety that comes from avoiding everything, but the kind that comes from having a nervous system that can regulate itself.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

Stephen Porges, the father of polyvagal theory, talks about neuroception. That's the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger without your conscious input. When your neuroception is calibrated toward danger, your striatum will keep encoding avoidance. But when you start giving your nervous system cues of safety, something shifts.

It's slow. I won't pretend it's fast. But it's real.

One thing that helps is pendulation. This is a term from Peter Levine's work. It means moving between a state of activation and a state of regulation, gently, without pushing. You notice the fear in your body. You feel it. Then you find a place of safety, even if it's just the feeling of your feet on the floor. You go back and forth. And over time, your striatum starts to learn that you can be with discomfort without needing to avoid it.

Another approach is titration. This means approaching what you've been avoiding in very small doses. Not all at once. Not flooding yourself. Just tiny exposures that your nervous system can handle. Each time you do this successfully, your striatum gets a new piece of data: "This didn't kill me. Maybe I don't need to avoid it so hard."

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry explore how trauma shapes the brain and what we can do to heal. It's a gentle, accessible read that doesn't shame you for your survival strategies.

The open ending

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I wish I did. But the truth is, rewiring the striatum is a long game. Some days you'll feel like you're making progress. Other days you'll feel like you're right back where you started.

That's okay.

Your striatum learned to avoid for good reasons. It kept you alive. And now, with time and patience and the right support, it can learn new things. It can learn that safety exists. That not every cue means danger. That you can be present in your body without being overwhelmed.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Maybe there was a flicker of hope. Maybe there was a tightening. Maybe both. That's okay. That's your nervous system doing what it does.

Sit with that. Don't try to fix it. Just notice it. That's the beginning of the rewiring.

  • Waking the Tiger (paid link)
